data.body:
PLAN REVIEW · CONTAINER · [G4] Child consistency — pairing (parent 6f2d ⟶ child b4af WS-EVAL-EXISTING), cross-checked against the complete authoritative 2-child roster [WS-DOC (4024) + WS-EVAL-EXISTING (b4af)] — VERDICT: CLEAN (no NEW findings). Re-affirms the prior G4 recording on this pairing after a fresh full 7-mode re-check.

Scope note: per the epic body's "REMAINING (this epic's only live deliverables)" line and the SCOPE-DECISION comments on 6f2d, the authoritative residual scope is exactly WS-DOC + WS-EVAL-EXISTING (WS-VISUAL-EVAL split into its own epic yip-gem-teak/7013; WS-MIGRATE/WS-PROMPTS/WS-CLEANUP/WS-VISUAL/WS-VALIDATE/WS-INTENT-ALIGN all DONE via closed sibling epics 5766/72d5/e6d9). So the 7-mode cross-child check runs WS-EVAL-EXISTING against exactly one live sibling: WS-DOC (4024).

7-mode audit (all CLEAN):
- Implicit shared state — CLEAN. Both children touch the eval seam, but the division is BUILD (b4af) vs DOCUMENT (4024). b4af PRODUCES the seam (scorer registry, run_eval(), eval-discipline/eval-live CI, datasets/fixtures); 4024 READS what b4af produces ("reflects WS-EVAL-EXISTING as built"). No undeclared shared mutable artifact; b4af does not silently depend on anything 4024 mutates.
- Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN. The two-tier eval model is described identically on both sides: zero-cost deterministic gate on every PR (b4af AC4 / 4024 AC4) + LLM-judge tier OFF the PR critical path — workflow_dispatch + weekly schedule, non-blocking, response-cached, concurrency-capped, fork-safe, spend-cap-guided (b4af AC5 / 4024 AC4). Tiers, triggers, and rails agree — no contradiction.
- Scope overlap — CLEAN. Building the seam + finishing the run machinery (b4af) vs documenting it (4024) is a clean labor split, not duplication. b4af claims no documentation deliverable; 4024 claims no build deliverable.
- Dependency gap — CLEAN (no missing producer). b4af is itself the producer the sibling depends on; b4af's own inbound dependency is the closed/DONE engine epic e6d9 + the migrated reviewers — not the live sibling. The un-declared sequencing of 4024-after-b4af is an ORDERING concern owned by the 4024 pairing, not a producer-absence here.
- Consumer impact — CLEAN/deferred. Per the epic's own agent-vs-single-turn bright line, G4 defers code-grounded consumer-impact to E4/G1G2/A1; no plan-level consumer breakage introduced by b4af against 4024.
- Residual references — CLEAN. b4af names no descoped/relocated workstreams as if live. The internal arithmetic reconciles: b4af's "all 7 specs" = 4 EXISTING (code-quality + plan-review finder/verifier/isf) + 3 NEW (completion-verifier, ticket-quality, spec-alignment). The "run_eval() unwired stub / scorer names inert / eval-live placeholder" premise is grounded. No stale references.
- Ordering violation — NO NEW FINDING from b4af's vantage. The only cross-child ordering binding is WS-DOC's AC4 "(consistent with WS-EVAL-EXISTING)" / Scope "reflects WS-EVAL-EXISTING as built", which obligates the CONSUMER (4024) to be sequenced after b4af's eval-seam delivery. That binding lives on the 4024 side and is ALREADY RECORDED as a G4 ordering finding on the 4024 pairing (timestamps 1782579130344411001 / 1782579768997254001 / 1782579813103394001). b4af is the producer and carries no independent ordering obligation, so re-charging the same interaction here would be double-counting. Noted for completeness, not emitted as a new finding.

No NEW G4 finding emitted for this pairing: empty findings list. (The single live cross-child interaction — the un-sequenced "document-the-seam-as-built" binding — is correctly owned by, and already flagged on, the WS-DOC (4024) pairing.)
